data.title: FTC broadens Microsoft probe, queries competitors on cloud, AI: report
data.description:
The U.S. Federal Trade Commission is speeding up scrutiny of Microsoft (MSFT) as part of an ongoing investigation to check if the company illegally monopolizes parts of the enterprise software market with its cloud software and AI services, including Copilot, Bloomberg News reported.

In recent weeks, the regulator has issued civil investigative demands to companies that compete with Microsoft in the business software and cloud computing markets. The demands feature queries on Microsoft's licensing and other business practices, the report added, citing people with knowledge of the matter. At least half a dozen companies have received the requests.

Microsoft and the FTC did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

The FTC is searching for evidence that Microsoft makes it difficult for customers to use Windows, Office, and other products on rival cloud services. The agency is also asking for information on Microsoft’s bundling of AI, security, and identity software into other products, including Windows and Office, the report noted.

However, no final decisions have been made yet.

Since complaints came from customers and competitors about its licensing practices, Microsoft has made some changes aimed at easing policies and, in particular, helping smaller European cloud providers more easily host Microsoft products, the report added. Last year, Microsoft signed an agreement with a group representing European cloud providers that takes more steps to address the concerns.

Microsoft has noted that some of its products are not fully interoperable with competing clouds because the technology underneath some features is different, the report added.

The investigation into licensing restrictions relates to rules changes Microsoft first introduced in 2019. Those changes also have been scrutinized by U.K. regulators, and many of the questions in the FTC demand appear to echo that work, according to the report.

The FTC sent Microsoft a demand for information in late 2024, the news outlet previously reported. The document requested the company to provide data about its AI operations, including the cost to train models and receive data, going as far back as 2016. The regulator was looking for details about Microsoft data centers, its struggles to find enough computing power to meet customer demand, and the company’s software licensing practices, the report added.

Most of the questions were about Microsoft’s licensing practices. About a third of them focused on the tech giant's AI business and reflected concerns that Microsoft canceled some of its own work after investing in OpenAI and leaning heavily on its software, ending potential competition, the report noted.

Former FTC chief Lina Khan had launched the probe into Microsoft during the Biden administration. The investigation is now in the hands of FTC Chairman Andrew Ferguson during Donald Trump's second term.

data.title: Cooper-Standard outlines double-digit EBITDA margin target for 2026 while accelerating China growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cooper-Standard Holdings Inc. (CPS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jeffrey Edwards highlighted that "in 2025, we continued to deliver world-class results in terms of product quality, program management and service for our customers," citing a 99% green product quality scorecard and a 98% green program launch scorecard. He noted the company achieved its "best year ever in terms of employee safety" with a safety incident rate of 0.24 per 200,000 hours worked and emphasized $64 million in cost savings from operational efficiencies and supply chain initiatives, as well as $18 million in year-over-year savings from salary reduction actions. Edwards stated, "we achieved a solid 24% improvement in operating income for the year."
  • Edwards announced that Cooper-Standard secured $298 million in net new business awards in 2025 and described the year as "our best operational performance in company history," delivering results "above our original operating plan and at the high end of the updated guidance range we gave you at the end of October."
  • CFO Jonathan Banas reported, "Fourth quarter 2025 sales totaled $672 million, an increase of 1.8% versus the fourth quarter of 2024" despite a customer supply chain disruption. He stated, "adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter 2025 was $34.9 million or 5.2% of sales," and "on a U.S. GAAP basis, we generated net income of $3.3 million in the fourth quarter."
  • Banas added, "full year 2025, our sales totaled $2.74 billion, an increase of 0.4% versus 2024," with adjusted EBITDA at $209.7 million for the year. He noted, "full year net loss significantly improved to $4.2 million from a net loss of $78.7 million in 2024."

Outlook

  • Edwards provided 2026 guidance for "increased profitability and for further margin expansion, leveraging an increase in sales of around 3% based on the most recent industry production outlook." He expressed confidence that the company will reach "our near-term strategic target of double-digit EBITDA margin for the full year in 2026."
  • Edwards stated, "the first quarter likely to be the weakest in terms of margins and cash flow, but building steadily throughout the remaining 3 quarters of the year."
  • Edwards indicated expectations to "reduce our net leverage ratio to something in the range of 2x or lower" between 2026 and 2028, and to "triple our total sales to Chinese OEMs globally over the next 5 years."

Financial Results

  • Banas reported that adjusted net loss for the fourth quarter of 2025 was $31 million or $1.73 per diluted share, compared to an adjusted net loss of $2.9 million in the fourth quarter of 2024. He stated, "for the full year 2025, our sales totaled $2.74 billion," and "adjusted EBITDA for the full year 2025 came in at $209.7 million compared to $180.7 million for the full year 2024."
  • Banas highlighted positive free cash flow of $44.6 million in the fourth quarter and $16.3 million for the full year. He noted, "as of December 31, 2025, we had cash on hand of $191.7 million and an additional $160.9 million of availability on our revolving credit facility."
  • He also mentioned plans for a potential refinancing, targeting a transaction in the near future.

Q&A

  • Kirk Ludtke, Imperial Capital: Asked about the $90 million lean initiative in the bridge to $280 million of adjusted EBITDA. Jonathan Banas responded, "it's business as usual for the team. They do this very, very well...nothing in and of itself is unusual within that $90 million."
  • Ludtke followed up on volume, mix, and price impacts from new products. CEO Edwards confirmed all net new business is included, stating, "as we head into '26, we've already identified well above 90% of all of that."
  • Michael Ward, Citigroup: Queried about production schedules and the F-Series. Edwards said, "there's a potential increase of 60,000 units if those 150,000 increase year-over-year happen," and described current schedules as "sticking."
  • Ward questioned the timing of refinancing. Banas stated, "we certainly would prefer to get something done prior to the first lien and third lien notes coming current...certainly, we don't want to have that added pressure of the notes coming current."
  • Nathan Jones, Stifel: Asked about layering of net new business. Edwards explained, "the $300 million that we booked in '25...typically take a couple of years, call it between 2 and 3 years on average to roll into the portfolio."
  • Jones requested color on revenue guidance and free cash flow. Edwards said, "if we get more volume than what is being predicted by S&P...then these numbers are going to be a lot better."
  • Brian DiRubbio, Baird: Sought clarity on China revenue mix. Edwards clarified, "our focus over the course of the next 3 years will be to increase the percentage of our Chinese OEM business in China to that number."
  • DiRubbio also probed on contract protections and risk. Edwards responded, "we've had 0 incidents of any issues with intellectual property."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised detailed questions about margin drivers, China strategy, refinancing timing, and production schedules, displaying a neutral to slightly cautious tone, especially around volume assumptions and free cash flow outlook.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, emphasizing operational achievements and future growth. Edwards remarked, "we are confident that the combination of increasing operating efficiencies and the ramp-up of higher-margin business will enable us to hit our near-term strategic target of double-digit EBITDA margin."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone remains probing but less focused on acute disruption risks, while management's confidence has increased, buoyed by year-end achievements and clarity around future initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language has shifted from caution about short-term disruptions in Q3 to a more optimistic outlook for 2026, with explicit targets for margin expansion and leverage reduction.
  • Strategic focus has intensified on China, with measurable targets for revenue mix and accelerated growth, while lean initiatives remain a core driver.
  • Analysts' questions have evolved from concerns about immediate production disruptions to more strategic inquiries about new business ramp and the sustainability of margin gains.
  • Management's tone has shifted from defensive (addressing Q4 headwinds) to assertive, citing operational records and clear paths to future profitability.
  • Key metric changes include improved net income, higher free cash flow, and a notable improvement in adjusted EBITDA margin year-over-year.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged potential risks related to volume and mix, customer production schedules, and tariffs, with Edwards stating, "volume and mix is always the number 1 answer to that question."
  • The company cited ongoing monitoring of debt markets and refinancing timing as a focus area.
  • Analysts expressed concern about the pace of revenue conversion from China and the durability of margin expansion amid global market uncertainties.

Final Takeaway

Cooper-Standard management underscored a record year for operational excellence, marked by robust cost controls, net new business wins, and progress in China. The company projects double-digit EBITDA margin for 2026 and aims to triple sales to Chinese OEMs over five years, underpinned by ongoing lean initiatives and expanding innovation in high-growth segments. Management's outlook for 2026 is optimistic, expecting continued margin expansion, positive free cash flow, and strategic leverage reduction, with China positioned as a central driver for future growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Regencell announces one-year extension to insider lock-up commitments
data.description:
• Regencell Bioscience (RGC), a Hong Kong-based biopharma focused on traditional Chinese medicine, announced on Friday that its directors and employees have agreed to further extend lock-up expirations related to their previously granted stock options.
  • As a result, all employees and directors with those stock options will see their lock-up undertakings extending by another 12 months until Apr. 20, 2027, Regencell (RGC) said in a regulatory filing.
  • Previously, their lock-up commitments and related extensions ran until April 20, 2026. The revised lock-up commitments are applicable to 82% to 92% of directors’ shares and vested share options, respectively, and 100% of employee shares and vested share options, according to the company.
  • Regencell (RGC) saw its share price skyrocketing to $21 in 2025, with the company attributing the volatility to a short squeeze.

data.title: Goldman sees further upside in the energy rally, flagging 10 buy-rated stocks
data.description:
Goldman Sachs said the sharp rally in U.S. energy equities this year does not mark the end of the trade, identifying 10 Buy-rated stocks that it believes still offer above-average upside despite the sector’s strong outperformance.

The S&P 500 Energy (XLE) sector has climbed about 23% year to date, compared with a roughly 1% gain for the broader market, as investors rotated into cyclicals amid firmer oil prices, improved growth expectations, and tighter-than-expected supply balances.

The bank said the move has been driven by positive GDP revisions, a broader rotation out of technology, and geopolitical uncertainty supporting crude prices. Goldman continues to anchor valuations to a mid-cycle framework of $70 per barrel Brent and $3.75 per MMBtu Henry Hub and estimates its 10 highlighted names offer an average total return potential of about 19% from current levels.

The list spans refining, global oils, exploration and production, midstream, oilfield services, and power generation.

In refining, HF Sinclair (DINO) is favored for its balance sheet strength, non-refining earnings contributions, and capital return profile. Among global oils and upstream names, Goldman highlighted ConocoPhillips (COP) for its expected free cash flow inflection as major projects ramp up, while EQT Corporation (EQT), Viper Energy (VNOM), and Diamondback Energy (FANG) are considered offering attractive risk-reward on the back of low-cost inventory and shareholder return frameworks.

In midstream and LNG, Kinder Morgan (KMI), Cheniere Energy (LNG), and Golar LNG (GLNG) are viewed as offering earnings visibility through contracted cash flows and project backlogs. In oilfield services, Halliburton (HAL) is considered positioned to benefit from improving international activity and margin expansion. In power generation, Vistra (VST) is expected to see upside from additional power purchase agreements and stable cash flows from its contracted fleet.

Price targets imply double-digit upside for most of the names, with Cheniere Energy, Golar LNG, and Vistra offering among the highest total return potential on Goldman’s estimates.

data.title: Flowers Foods outlines multiyear brand and operational review while targeting traditional loaf recovery
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Flowers Foods (FLO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ryals McMullian opened the call by highlighting progress on business transformation, stating, "Led by the strong performance of our leading brands and disciplined execution of efficiency initiatives, we produced results at the high end of our 2025 guidance range." He noted that the 2026 guidance "does reflect ongoing category challenges, 1 fewer week, inflationary pressures, but also additional investments in our leading brands."
  • McMullian announced a comprehensive review of operations, including "brand portfolio, supply chain and financial strategy, to strengthen execution and position our business to reignite top line growth and expand margins over time."
  • McMullian emphasized the challenge in the traditional loaf segment, specifying, "The real issue for us is traditional loaf where we under-index, and that has been underperforming the category."
  • CFO Diego Scaglione described the review as "a measured approach," adding, "It's a thoughtful and broad review. It's really not intended to be just for this year, but it's a multiyear process."
  • Scaglione addressed dividend concerns, stating, "We remain committed, and I think we mentioned this in the prepared remarks as well, to our strong balance sheet and we recognize the benefit of the investment-grade rating."

Outlook

  • Management stated that 2026 guidance reflects "ongoing category challenges, 1 fewer week, inflationary pressures, but also additional investments in our leading brands." The company is conducting a comprehensive review, but is "just in the early stretches right now."
  • Scaglione indicated that the guidance range for 2026 is "down roughly about 180 basis points to slightly up, effectively flat," and expects the category to be down 4% from a headwind, with "extra week adds about 150 basis points of pressure."
  • No explicit figures for EPS or revenue guidance were provided in the transcript.

Financial Results

  • No specific quarterly financial results, such as revenue, EPS, or cash flow, were explicitly disclosed in the transcript.
  • McMullian reported results "at the high end of our 2025 guidance range."
  • Simple Mills sales for the year were reported at "$214 million," which was below the full year guidance range of "$221 million to $223 million."
  • Simple Mills' EBITDA margin was noted to have dipped to "11% EBITDA margin in 4Q versus 16% in the first 3 quarters."

Q&A

  • Stephen Robert Powers, Deutsche Bank: Asked for details on the operational review's scope and timeline. CEO McMullian responded that the company is "in the early innings of the review" and is focused on "how do we reinvigorate Nature's Own," as well as supply chain efficiency. CFO Scaglione added, "It's really not intended to be just for this year, but it's a multiyear process."
  • James Salera, Stephens Inc.: Sought clarity on stabilizing the traditional loaf category. McMullian explained, "It's the key to everything for us... It's our largest brand, in Nature's Own. It's also the #1 brand," and underscored demographic and macroeconomic shifts affecting consumption.
  • Max Andrew Gumport, BNP Paribas: Questioned the decision not to cut the dividend despite a high payout ratio and leverage. Scaglione reiterated, "dividend is a function of our discussion clearly with our Board... our intent is to plan to provide that detail in the upcoming quarters."
  • Gumport also asked about outlook assumptions regarding SNAP, Supreme Court impacts, refinancing, and market share gains. Scaglione responded, "we recognized a reduction in EBT purchases," and is monitoring that channel. On refinancing, "rates are going to be slightly higher than the rate of the bond that we're taking out, but we feel confident that's going to be part of our process."
  • Mitchell Pinheiro, Sturdivant & Co.: Asked about supply chain review and possible bakery consolidation. McMullian confirmed it's "part of the review," and added, "we've closed several bakeries over the last few years."
  • Pinheiro also inquired about the move to regional P&L responsibility for DSD. McMullian said, "there was a need for greater accountability closer to the individual markets."
  • Gumport followed up on maintenance CapEx and Simple Mills' 4Q performance. Scaglione cited maintenance CapEx at "around $2 million, plus or minus, per bakery per year." McMullian attributed Simple Mills' 4Q miss to "inventory deloading related to 1 distributor" and an issue with "coconut sugar."
  • Scott Marks, Jefferies: Asked about heightened reinvestment and competition. McMullian stated, "What's different this time...is when you look at our portfolio, when you look at our market share performance, the clear issue is in traditional loaf, and that primarily means Nature's Own."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts repeatedly pressed for details on the operational review, capital allocation, and traditional loaf stabilization, reflecting a slightly negative sentiment and skepticism about clarity and timing. They challenged management on the dividend and leverage, signaled by pointed questions about "why not cut the dividend today" and concerns about "risk of tripping your 3.75x covenant."
  • Management maintained a measured and defensive tone, often emphasizing that reviews are "in the early innings" and reiterating commitment to a "strong balance sheet." McMullian spoke confidently about brand investments but acknowledged challenges.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from optimism around innovation and category leadership to a more cautious, process-focused stance. Analyst questioning became more direct and critical, particularly about financial discipline and outlook details.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced a comprehensive review of operations, encompassing the brand portfolio, supply chain, and capital allocation, which was not a focus in the prior quarter.
  • While previous commentary centered on innovation and leveraging leading brands, the latest call shifted to confronting underperformance in the traditional loaf segment and addressing margin and leverage concerns.
  • Guidance language became more conservative, with explicit references to inflation, fewer weeks in the fiscal year, and increased investments.
  • Analyst questions intensified around dividend sustainability, leverage, and the clarity/timing of operational changes, compared to a more balanced tone in the previous quarter.
  • Management’s sentiment moved from forward-looking confidence about growth and innovation to a measured, cautious approach, frequently referencing "early stages" and "multiyear process."

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified "ongoing category challenges," inflation, and demographic shifts reducing traditional loaf demand.
  • Underperformance in traditional loaf, particularly Nature's Own, was flagged as the primary issue affecting operating leverage.
  • Dividend sustainability and leverage were highlighted as risks, with Scaglione noting that the payout will be evaluated as part of the strategic review.
  • Supply chain efficiency and potential bakery consolidation are under review to address cost base and operational flexibility.
  • Analysts expressed concern about the risk of covenant breaches, the impact of SNAP reductions, refinancing at higher rates, and the lack of explicit CapEx plans for the coming year.

Final Takeaway

Flowers Foods' Q4 2025 call signaled a pivotal shift as the company embarks on a multiyear review targeting operational, portfolio, and financial strategy improvements. Management is prioritizing a turnaround in its traditional loaf business, particularly Nature's Own, while balancing margin pressures, elevated leverage, and market headwinds. The company underscored its commitment to brand investment and operational efficiency, with ongoing review processes and capital allocation decisions to be clarified in future quarters. Investors should closely watch for updates on the comprehensive review, dividend policy, and performance in the traditional loaf segment as Flowers Foods navigates near-term challenges and lays the groundwork for sustainable growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: The widening gap between CPI and PPI is what truly matters for the stock market – strategist
data.description:
While the Consumer Price Index remains “fairly benign,” the inflation metric that truly matters for equity markets (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) is the widening gap between CPI and PPI, according to Gina Martin Adams, chief market strategist for HB Wealth.

Adams argued this spread has been “going the wrong way since October,” signaling potential trouble ahead for corporate profits and stock valuations.

 

 

 

The timing of this shift appears significant, the strategist noted. “It’s not likely a coincidence that October was also the latest peak in growth stocks and value has been on a tear since,” Adams said.

The rotation from growth (IVW) to value stocks (IVE) has closely tracked the deteriorating relationship between consumer and producer prices, suggesting investors are already pricing in concerns about corporate pricing power.

The implications for the broader market could be substantial if current trends persist. According to Adams, if “core producer prices continue to rise at a faster pace than core consumer prices, it may indicate fading pricing power.”

This dynamic threatens to undermine the optimistic outlook many analysts have held for corporate America.

The chief market strategist warned that sustained pressure from rising producer costs could “reverse consensus forecasts for broad S&P 500 (SP500) margin gains in the year ahead.”

For investors, the message is clear: headline CPI figures tell only part of the story, and the underlying spread between what companies pay and what consumers will bear may prove the more consequential indicator for equity returns, according to Adams.

ETFs that track the main U.S. indexes: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

data.title: Largest YTD losses among mid-cap software stocks with a profitability grades
data.description:
As AI continues to disrupt the software market, below is a list of the top 10 mid-cap software stocks with A profitability grades that have experienced the largest YTD losses. The list is arranged by their YTD performance as of February 2026.

The list is topped by Kyndryl Holdings (KD), with a YTD performance of -57.30%. Unity Software (U) and Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) are next, with Core Scientific (CORZR) and Doximity (DOCS) rounding out the rest of the top five.

Klaviyo (KVYO) and ServiceTitan (TTAN) also appear among the biggest decliners. Neptune Insurance Holdings (NP), Navan (NAVN) and Snap (SNAP) complete the top 10, all with YTD losses exceeding 40%.

Here is the list:

  1. Kyndryl Holdings (KD), YTD perf: -57.30%, Quant Rating: Strong Sell 1.36

  2. Unity Software (U), YTD perf: -55.51%, Quant Rating: Hold 2.74

  4. Core Scientific (CORZR), YTD perf: -46.15%, Quant Rating: Not Covered

data.title: AI-insulated? CEO Chesky thinks AI is 'the best thing to happen to Airbnb'
data.description:
The artificial intelligence bogeyman that has plagued the software as a service ((SAAS)) industry and fueled a bloodbath in the tech sector is unlikely to sabotage Airbnb (ABNB) even as the online travel agency ((OTA)) segment faces a seismic shift in search habits.

“From a business standpoint, AI is the best thing to ever happen to Airbnb,” the company’s CEO, Brian Chesky, said on CNBC.  

And Wall Street analysts seem to agree. 

“We expect trip planning will change in an AI world,” said Wells Fargo’s Ken Gawrelski. “But see the unique inventory of ABNB as providing a barrier to disintermediation.” This, Gawrelski believes, contrasts with peers like Booking Holdings (BKNG) and Expedia (EXPE), both of which are at a much greater risk to commoditized chain hotel inventory and a greater share of their traffic driven from search referrals.

In Airbnb’s (ABNB) recent quarterly results – which showed double-digit gains in most metrics and beat Wall Street’s expectations—adjusted EBITDA in 2026 is expected to be unchanged from last year due to increased investments in marketing, product, and technology, the latter of which translates into greater integration of AI, a strategy the company is confident will translate into a superior platform and drive traffic.

“We’re not a software layer that AI can potentially do more efficiently; most of Airbnb’s business is in the real world, and we think Airbnb/AI is a tailwind,” Chesky assured, reframing AI not as a risk to Airbnb but as an opportunity, noting that chatbots are likely to enhance top-of-funnel discovery.

“Traffic that comes from chatbots converts at a higher rate than traffic that comes from Google,” he added.

And since these large language models ((LLMs) developed by OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic are not proprietary, Airbnb (ABNB) will be able to “build everything everyone else will have if we use their models."

“We believe specialization will win in travel because if somebody wants to find an Airbnb or have a trip, we can take their model, the same model they use, we can post train it, and tune it based on our million interactions,” Chesky added.

Based on the stock’s post-earnings performance, investors don’t appear overly concerned about AI risks to Airbnb (ABNB) either, with the share price jump echoing RBC Capital’s Brad Erickson’s view that the company is “focused on mastering AI for its own first-party benefit.”

By comparison, Expedia (EXPE) shares remain defensive as the AI risk is more acute for OTAs, evidenced by the outsized reaction earlier this month to an AI-fueled carnage in the software sector. Shares of Expedia (EXPE), Booking Holdings (BKNG), and Tripadvisor (TRIP) all suffered double-digit losses while Airbnb (ABNB) remained largely insulated from the worst of the meltdown.  

So as AI reshuffles the online travel hierarchy, Airbnb’s differentiated inventory and direct customer relationships may prove more durable than traditional search-dependent models.
